nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo Database): Beef Hor Fun and component references
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for black bean sauce beef hor fun. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for stir-fried beef hor fun and black bean sauce dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for flat rice noodles (USDA #20113) and cooked beef (USDA #13365). The input record's per-100g values (energy 150 kcal, protein 7 g, fat 6 g, carbohydrate 17 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed values for this composite dish are not individually reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Rice noodles (flat, cooked) analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black bean sauce beef hor fun as a composite dish. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for flat rice noodles (hor fun / rice vermicelli, cooked), which reports GI ~61 (medium). The black bean sauce, beef, and vegetables contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and are not expected to materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (61 × 85) / 100 ≈ 52 (carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 17 g/100 g × 500 g = 85 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from black bean sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce combination; 360 mg sodium per 100 g equates to approximately 1800 mg per standard 500 g serving, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced sauce quantities, substitute regular soy sauce with low-sodium soy sauce, and limit portion size to no more than 200–250 g to keep sodium intake below 900 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Flat rice noodles (hor fun) are refined carbohydrates with a medium-to-high GI (~61); a 500 g serving delivers approximately 85 g of carbohydrate with only 4 g of dietary fibre, producing a high glycemic load (~52) and significant postprandial glucose excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request additional non-starchy vegetables (bok choy, bean sprouts) to increase fibre, and consider substituting hor fun with konjac noodles or shirataki to substantially reduce glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 1.6 g/100 g and cholesterol at 16 mg/100 g from beef, combined with high sodium (360 mg/100 g) and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), present a moderate cardiovascular risk profile; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 8 g saturated fat and 1800 mg sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request leaner beef cuts or substitute with chicken breast or tofu, reduce sauce volume to lower sodium, and avoid adding extra oil during preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which can amplify pro-inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokines; the dish also lacks meaningful anti-inflammatory micronutrients (fibre 0.8 g/100 g, vitamin C 1 mg/100 g).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with fish (salmon, mackerel) or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid load, and request additional vegetables such as broccoli or bok choy to introduce anti-inflammatory phytonutrients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Bean Sauce Beef Hor Fun (豉汁牛肉河粉) is a popular Singaporean Chinese stir-fried noodle dish featuring wide, silky flat rice noodles (hor fun or he fen) tossed with tender sliced beef and a savoury, umami-rich black bean sauce made from fermented black beans (douchi), garlic, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. The dish is prepared by wok-frying the noodles over high heat to achieve slight charring and wok hei (breath of the wok), then combining with marinated beef and the black bean sauce mixture. The texture is soft and slippery from the rice noodles, with tender beef and a deeply savoury, slightly salty sauce. It is a staple at Singaporean hawker centres, zi char restaurants, and coffee shops, commonly served as a main course at lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 150 kcal, 7 g protein, 6 g fat (1.6 g saturated), and 17 g carbohydrate with 0.8 g dietary fibre and 360 mg sodium. The high sodium content from the multi-sauce profile makes this dish a concern for hypertension patients. The refined carbohydrate base with a medium GI (~61) and high glycemic load per serving (~52) makes it unsuitable for uncontrolled type 2 diabetes. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not halal due to beef content. Common allergens include soy, gluten (from wheat-based soy sauce), and shellfish (from oyster sauce).

GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database, flat rice noodles). Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated), potassium, B-vitamin panel (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B6, B12, folate), fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E), and mineral micronutrients (phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), and moisture data for this composite stir-fried dish are not individually reported in HPB Foodo (foodo.sg, accessed 2024-01-01) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation; these fields are therefore set to -1 to indicate data unavailability rather than a confirmed zero value. The input record listed 'primary_cuisine' as 'Filipino' and ethnic tags including 'Coffee' and 'Cake', which are clearly erroneous for this dish; these have been corrected to Singaporean Chinese / Cantonese. The canonical English name has been corrected from 'Back Bean Sauce Beef Horfun' (typographical error in input) to 'Black Bean Sauce Beef Hor Fun'. The dish is not confirmed halal as beef sourcing and sauce ingredients (oyster sauce, fermented black beans) require halal certification verification. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on flat rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100 g from the input record; this is a plausible trace level from cooking oil and is retained.
